item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've got to admit, I lost it at:

After investigators reviewed the security cameras and discovered footage of housecats with handguns in their mouths in contrast to the witness reports, the Foundation was notified.

+1, right there. That said, I agree the transcript is a bit weak and seems to only exist to show off the cats arguing.

I think it'd be more interesting to have an interview transcript with Marianne Baker, something that reveals why they robbed the bank (probably to help pay for her care?).

item.title: Good for a lighthearted article
item.content:encoded:
See title. Enjoyed it, funny idea. Could use expanding, add some dialogue between researchers and the cats/owner. Doesn't need a lot of info since it's not a "serious" type article. Gotta agree with Boogey about the MTF tho. Agree with Radioactive about the cat's not sticking out much, could use more interaction with Foundation.

But since it's just supposed to be a short, fun article I'll give it a +1, and hope for more work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't mind the concept here at all, I think the idea has some good entertainment potential. But I felt like the execution was kinda rough, or there were details I got hung up on at least which kept me from really enjoying it.

A particular pet peeve of mine is superfluous creation of MTFs for duties that regular on-site personnel would already do. I can see in this case that it was a way for the author to slip another reference into the work; but in-universe, it's the Foundation explicitly creating a Task Force, with the dedicated elite specialists vibes that carries, for the job of domestic care duties for six pet cats. Like food dish and litterbox are too much for an average Containment Technician to handle.

So I like the premise of this article, but I guess I was taken out of it too much by a number of little things that bugged me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I truly liked the idea of a criminal cat syndicate committing bank robberies because they saw a bunch of movies like that. But the interview didn't add anything IMO. All there is is just the cats getting upset and attacking each other because they got caught. It wasn't funny to me and the cats didn't stick out enough for a dialogue log.

But everything else to me was good. No-vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gotta say I dislike the formatting, it feel off. You're lucky i like the idea of criminal cat gangs so much. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love this! I don't know much about Reservoir Dogs or any of the other franchises this is inspired by but this SCP makes me want to find out. The dialogue is written very well, although the article itself could do with some clarification with how the "fight" or any other physical interactions with each other work. Do most humans see the human versions of the cats fighting like humans, or do they phase through each other and fight like cats? It would be nice to see some clarification on that, otherwise amazing job writing this and including so many references, even in the numbers.

+1

item.title: SCP-4723
item.content:encoded:
A silly idea written as a big homage to Tarantino and Reservoir Dogs. I'm pleased with this one but it is a bit reliant on such knowledge, so if this one goes down I won't be particularly devastated.
Crit thanks to:

  • Rounderhouse
  • Ellie3
  • DrChandra
  • aismallard
  • Popsioak
  • Gamesofmax
  • and others
» SCP-4723 references
« SCP-4723 references

Reservoir Cats: obv. play on Reservoir Dogs

SCP-4723: This one's got quite a bit to it.

  • 47 is 74 backwards, the year the track "Jungle Boogie" from Pulp Fiction came out.
  • 23 has a lot of significance in different ways as well. In this case, 23 is 1 + 9 + 9 + 4 (1994), the year Reservoir Dogs came out.

Koppa-23: Koppa is the Greek letter for Q. The 23rd letter of the alphabet is T. Hence, "QT" for Quentin Tarantino.

("Jungle Boogie"): Another reference to Pulp Fic's music piece.

six […] housepets: Six main characters of RDogs.

human males in buisness suits: Reference to the attire of the crew from RDogs.

64-year-old: 64 + 23 (chosen for 'importance') = 87. The movie 'City on Fire' the influence for RDogs, came out in 1987.

Ma Baker: Reference to the Boney M song.

Breed charts: All those breeds of cats usually have certain colors. They match (mostly) with the colors of the RDogs crew. Additionally, they all have a certain motif: country names.

Lawrence Nimitz: Play on "Lawrence Dimmick", the in-universe real name for Mr. White.

David Gafver: Gafver is a play on gaffer's tape.

3/28: The length of the song "Stuck in the Middle with You" used in RDogs is 3:28. (also trivia: the page for the draft was titled "stuck-in-the-middle-with-meow".)

Nash County Bank: Refers to Marvin Nash, the police officer captured by the RDogs crew.

$570,000: The amount of times I've seen RDogs I forgot where I read this, but that was to be the original production budget of RDogs.

Charged for two counts of armed robbery.: If I remember correctly, this is the amount of times Eddie Bunker - the actor for Mr. Blue - was charged for that particular crime.

4/4: Technically (emphasis on technically) April 4th is 4 days after March 31st. 31 + 4 = 35, a nod to Tarantino's love of 35mm film to this day.

"how it happened for the dogs.": Reference to RDogs, implying that they've seen the movie.

"We're supposed to be professionals.": Reference to Steve Buscemi's similar line ("We're supposed to be fucking professionals!") in RDogs.

It's a lot, admittedly, and I don't expect a lot of people to get all of them. The article isn't reliant on them (hopefully) either.
